posts.text: Informative NYT oped by a Navy veteran on the challenge and necessity of reopening the Strait of Hormuz: “Should the regime survive the war with the power to close Hormuz at will…any declarations of victory by the United States will ring hollow.” nytimes.com/2026/03/19/opini…

posts.text:
Israel is targeting the Islamic Republic’s pillars of repression. The question is whether these are structural pillars, or men who can be easily replaced. The regime’s deep unpopularity and isolation have hardened its resolve. CNN with @andersoncooper

Video

Ali Larijani had a track record of mass repression as well as decades of domestic and foreign policy experience. He will not be easily replaced. Discussion w @kaitlancollins on the state of the military, economic, and political war between the US and Iran.

posts.text: What began as a war of choice has morphed into a war of necessity for the security of the global economy. My conversation with @nprfreshair on Iran’s state and society, the Strait of Hormuz, and Trump’s predicament. npr.org/2026/03/17/nx-s1-575…

posts.text: 9 Trump's dilemma is whether he can accept ending this war having only degraded Iran's capabilities--leaving the country in the hands of a Khamenei whose worldview is just as dogmatic as the man he killed, and three decades younger. theatlantic.com/internationa…
posts.quote: 9 Trump's dilemma is whether he can accept ending this war having only degraded Iran's capabilities--leaving the country in the hands of a Khamenei whose worldview is just as dogmatic as the man he killed, and three decades younger. theatlantic.com/internationa…

posts.text: 8 For the US the war--less than two weeks in--thus far appears a military success but a political failure. The campaign has effectively degraded the regime's malign capacities, but as of now it has seemingly strengthened the regime’s cohesion and not changed its character.
posts.quote: 8 For the US the war--less than two weeks in--thus far appears a military success but a political failure. The campaign has effectively degraded the regime's malign capacities, but as of now it has seemingly strengthened the regime’s cohesion and not changed its character.

posts.text: 6 Siamak Namazi, a US citizen held hostage for eight years by the IRGC, described Taeb as “the most hard-line of the hard-line” and “one of the most evil figures in the Islamic Republic.” Namazi believes that Taeb, with Mojtaba’s blessing, has been behind the taking of hostages.

Ali Larijani had a track record of mass repression as well as decades of domestic and foreign policy experience. He will not be easily replaced. Discussion w @kaitlancollins on the state of the military, economic, and political war between the US and Iran.

What began as a war of choice has morphed into a war of necessity for the security of the global economy. My conversation with @nprfreshair on Iran’s state and society, the Strait of Hormuz, and Trump’s predicament. npr.org/2026/03/17/nx-s1-575…

9 Trump's dilemma is whether he can accept ending this war having only degraded Iran's capabilities--leaving the country in the hands of a Khamenei whose worldview is just as dogmatic as the man he killed, and three decades younger. theatlantic.com/internationa…

8 For the US the war--less than two weeks in--thus far appears a military success but a political failure. The campaign has effectively degraded the regime's malign capacities, but as of now it has seemingly strengthened the regime’s cohesion and not changed its character.

6 Siamak Namazi, a US citizen held hostage for eight years by the IRGC, described Taeb as “the most hard-line of the hard-line” and “one of the most evil figures in the Islamic Republic.” Namazi believes that Taeb, with Mojtaba’s blessing, has been behind the taking of hostages.
